title: Seeds of Change: A Paleolithic Adventure
description: Play as Kara, a young gatherer deciding whether her people should continue the old nomadic ways or risk the first steps toward agriculture.

coverImage.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

teacherNotes: This deep-tree adventure focuses on human-environment interaction, the risks of innovation, cooperation, food surplus, and the transition from hunter-gatherer life to settled farming communities.
reviewFocus: how the shift from hunting and gathering to agriculture changed survival, settlement, labor, and social organization
topicQuestion: how the Neolithic Revolution changed human life

start.title: The Edge of Survival

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

start.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: You are Kara, a young gatherer in a Paleolithic band that has always survived by moving with the seasons.
The herds have changed their path. Wild berries are harder to find. Your younger brother Tavi walks beside the fire with one hand pressed against his stomach.
Near the river, you notice something strange. Plants you saw weeks ago have grown back thicker in the same patch of damp soil.
You kneel and roll a seed between your fingers. For the first time, you wonder if food has to be chased forever.

choices.text: Study the plants before telling anyone.
choices.hint: Careful observation may reveal a pattern in the environment.

choices.text: Bring seeds back to camp and experiment in secret.
choices.hint: Innovation can begin quietly, but working alone is risky.

choices.text: Tell Elder Nima what you noticed.
choices.hint: A respected elder may help or resist change.

choices.text: Ignore the plants and follow the hunters.
choices.hint: The old ways are familiar, but food is becoming uncertain.

watch-river.title: Patterns in the Mud

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

watch-river.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: You return to the river before sunrise. Mist hangs over the water, and the mud is cool beneath your feet.
Seeds fall. Days pass. Green shoots rise. The strongest plants grow closest to the floodwater.
You do not have the word agriculture yet, but you are beginning to understand its first secret. Food may be grown where people choose to stay.

choices.text: Mark the places where seeds grow best.
choices.hint: Evidence may help you convince others.

choices.text: Ask your friend Sela to observe with you.
choices.hint: Shared knowledge can become shared action.

choices.text: Tell the whole group immediately.
choices.hint: The group may listen, but they may ask for proof.

secret-seeds.title: Seeds in Secret

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

secret-seeds.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: You hide seeds in a fold of animal skin and press them into damp soil near the river.
At first, nothing happens. Then tiny shoots break through the ground.
Your chest tightens with excitement. The land is answering you, but the plants are fragile and you are only one person.

choices.text: Protect the seedlings from animals.
choices.hint: Early farming requires protection and planning.

choices.text: Keep the experiment hidden until the plants grow taller.
choices.hint: More proof may help, but secrecy creates risk.

choices.text: Tell Tavi and ask him to help water the plants.
choices.hint: Family stakes may deepen Kara's commitment.

elder-warning.title: The Elder's Warning

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

elder-warning.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: Elder Nima listens without interrupting. Her face is lined by many hungry seasons.
She says, 'The land gives and takes. People who forget to move may become bones beside the river.'
Still, she does not dismiss you. She asks what proof you have.

choices.text: Promise to gather evidence before the next migration.
choices.hint: Evidence may persuade cautious leaders.

choices.text: Argue that the old ways are already failing.
choices.hint: Fear can persuade, but it can also divide the group.

choices.text: Ask Nima to teach you what she knows about plants.
choices.hint: Old knowledge may support a new idea.

old-road.title: The Old Road

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

old-road.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: You turn away from the river and follow the hunters.
Your father Orun walks beside you with his spear over his shoulder. He says survival means movement.
By nightfall, the hunters return with little meat. The old road still works, but not as well as it once did.

choices.text: Help the hunters track the herd more carefully.
choices.hint: Improved hunting may help the group survive.

choices.text: Suggest returning to the river after the hunt.
choices.hint: Kara may still move toward agriculture.

choices.text: Accept the old ways and keep moving.
choices.hint: Tradition may preserve unity, but hunger remains.

marked-ground.title: Proof in the Soil

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

marked-ground.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: You place stones beside the strongest plants and scratch marks into a flat piece of bark.
The best growth happens near the river where floodwater leaves dark soil behind.
The river valley is no longer just a place to pass through. It may be a place where people can stay.

choices.text: Create a small test field using the best soil.
choices.hint: This moves Kara closer to agriculture.

choices.text: Show the marked ground to Elder Nima.
choices.hint: Evidence may make the council listen.

choices.text: Study old flood marks before choosing a camp.
choices.hint: Understanding geography may prevent disaster.

sela-joins.title: Two Sets of Eyes

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

sela-joins.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: Sela laughs at first. Then she sees the pattern too.
Together, you track where seeds fall, where animals feed, and where the soil stays wet longest.
The idea feels less impossible now that someone else believes it.

choices.text: Ask Sela to help build barriers around the plants.
choices.hint: Protecting crops is an early farming challenge.

choices.text: Have Sela speak with younger members of the group.
choices.hint: Support within the group may grow.

choices.text: Prepare a careful argument for the council.
choices.hint: Persuasion may help the group choose settlement.

council-fire.title: The Council Fire

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

council-fire.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: That night, the group gathers around the fire. You speak with muddy hands and a shaking voice.
You explain that seeds can be planted, that river soil helps them grow, and that staying might create a steadier food supply.
Some faces brighten. Others harden with fear.

choices.text: Argue that farming could create a food surplus.
choices.hint: Surplus is a major turning point toward civilization.

choices.text: Suggest a temporary settlement as a test.
choices.hint: A compromise may avoid total risk.

choices.text: Ask the group to divide labor between hunting and planting.
choices.hint: This may balance old and new ways.

tense-council.title: Words Like Sparks

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

tense-council.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: Your words come out sharper than you meant. 'The old ways are failing,' you say. 'Tavi is starving. Others are too.'
The fire goes quiet. Your father looks away. Some agree with you, but others think you have insulted the ancestors.

choices.text: Apologize and explain your idea more carefully.
choices.hint: Repairing trust may help later.

choices.text: Stand firm and demand that the group stay.
choices.hint: Forceful leadership may deepen conflict.

choices.text: Ask Nima to speak for you.
choices.hint: A respected elder may calm the group.

nima-lessons.title: Old Knowledge, New Question

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

nima-lessons.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: Nima teaches you which plants return after rain, which roots poison the stomach, and which seeds birds fight over.
You realize your idea is not separate from the old ways. It grows from them.
Maybe innovation means carrying tradition into a new problem.

choices.text: Use Nima's knowledge to choose the strongest seeds.
choices.hint: This supports early domestication.

choices.text: Ask Nima to support you at council.
choices.hint: Elder support may change the group decision.

choices.text: Plan a small experiment rather than a full change.
choices.hint: Careful innovation lowers risk.

animal-tracks.title: Tracks in the Field

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

animal-tracks.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: In the morning, you find tracks pressed deep into the mud.
Some seedlings are bitten down to stubs. The plants can grow, but they cannot protect themselves.
Farming does not end hunger by magic. It creates new work.

choices.text: Build a thorn barrier around the plants.
choices.hint: Protection may improve the harvest.

choices.text: Ask hunters to guard the field at night.
choices.hint: This requires cooperation between old and new roles.

choices.text: Move the test field closer to camp.
choices.hint: Location choices matter in settlement planning.

hidden-field.title: The Secret Grows

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

hidden-field.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: The plants grow taller behind the reeds.
Each day, you feel more certain. Each night, you feel more afraid.
If the group moves before the plants mature, your proof disappears. If you reveal it too soon, they may laugh and leave anyway.

choices.text: Reveal the hidden field now.
choices.hint: Early honesty may build trust.

choices.text: Wait until the plants produce seeds.
choices.hint: More proof may come at a cost.

choices.text: Ask Tavi to help you convince your father first.
choices.hint: Family support may influence the group.

tavi-helps.title: Tavi's Small Hands

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

tavi-helps.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: Tavi carries water in a hollowed shell, spilling half before reaching the plants.
He laughs for the first time in days.
This is no longer only about an idea. It is about whether your family lives.

choices.text: Teach Tavi to water the plants each morning.
choices.hint: Routine supports settled life.

choices.text: Bring your father to see Tavi helping.
choices.hint: Family evidence may persuade Orun.

choices.text: Protect the plants before animals return.
choices.hint: Planning may prevent failure.

hard-hunt.title: The Hard Hunt

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

hard-hunt.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: You study broken grass, hoofprints, and droppings.
The hunters find a small herd near a dry streambed. The hunt succeeds, but barely.
There is meat tonight, not enough for many nights. Skill keeps you alive, but scarcity keeps following.

choices.text: Keep improving the group's hunting methods.
choices.hint: This strengthens the nomadic path.

choices.text: Use the successful hunt to buy time for river experiments.
choices.hint: Food from hunting may support farming trials.

choices.text: Argue that hunting alone is no longer enough.
choices.hint: This may create tension with hunters.

return-river.title: Back to the Green Place

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

return-river.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: You convince a few others to return to the river.
The plants are still there, bending in the wind. The sight feels like a question waiting for an answer.
This time, you are not alone.

choices.text: Begin a shared test field.
choices.hint: Cooperation supports agriculture.

choices.text: Call a council before taking action.
choices.hint: The whole group must decide.

choices.text: Use the river only as a temporary food stop.
choices.hint: This keeps the group between old and new ways.

long-migration.title: Farther From the River

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

long-migration.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: The group moves farther from the river valley.
The land becomes drier. The children walk more slowly. At night, people speak less.
Hunger makes every sound feel sharper.

choices.text: Encourage the group to turn back before it is too late.
choices.hint: Kara may still change the path.

choices.text: Search for new hunting grounds.
choices.hint: This continues the nomadic path.

choices.text: Stay loyal to the migration and say nothing.
choices.hint: Silence may avoid conflict, but danger grows.

young-listeners.title: The Younger Circle

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

young-listeners.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: Sela gathers the younger members of the group near the riverbank.
They listen as you show them the shoots, the damp soil, and the marked stones.
They have less fear of changing old patterns, but they have less power to decide.

choices.text: Ask them to help build and guard a test field.
choices.hint: Many hands can support a new system.

choices.text: Send them to persuade their families.
choices.hint: Support may spread through the group.

choices.text: Move ahead without the elders.
choices.hint: Youthful confidence may divide the camp.

river-memory.title: The River's Memory

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

river-memory.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: You study marks left by old floods on stones and tree trunks.
The river feeds the soil, but it can also swallow a camp.
A settlement will need more than seeds. It will need planning.

choices.text: Build shelters on higher ground.
choices.hint: Good settlement planning may prevent disaster.

choices.text: Dig small channels to guide water.
choices.hint: This is a step toward irrigation.

choices.text: Warn the council that the river must be respected.
choices.hint: Planning may influence the group decision.

surplus-dream.title: A Dream of Full Baskets

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

surplus-dream.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: You tell the group that if crops grow well, there may be more food than the group needs for one meal.
The word surplus does not exist around your fire, but the idea makes everyone quiet.
Extra food could mean fewer hungry nights. It could also mean arguments over who controls it.

choices.text: Propose shared storage for the whole group.
choices.hint: Shared storage may build trust.

choices.text: Ask strong families to guard the food.
choices.hint: Power over food may create hierarchy.

choices.text: Focus first on proving the harvest can happen.
choices.hint: Evidence may matter before rules.

trial-settlement.title: The Trial Settlement

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

trial-settlement.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: The group agrees to stay near the river for one season.
The shelters are still light enough to move, but the fires remain in the same place night after night.
You have been given time, but not trust.

choices.text: Build a careful farming plan for the season.
choices.hint: Planning may lead to success.

choices.text: Keep hunting and gathering while crops grow.
choices.hint: Balance may protect against failure.

choices.text: Push the group to become fully settled immediately.
choices.hint: Full commitment can bring reward or disaster.

divided-labor.title: Many Hands

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

divided-labor.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: The group begins dividing work.
Hunters still track animals. Gatherers collect wild plants. Others guard the field, carry water, shape tools, or repair shelters.
No one calls this job specialization yet, but different tasks are beginning to shape daily life.

choices.text: Create fair work rotations so no one feels trapped.
choices.hint: Fair systems build trust.

choices.text: Let the strongest families control the best jobs.
choices.hint: This may create hierarchy and conflict.

choices.text: Ask Nima to organize work using experience and need.
choices.hint: Elder leadership may stabilize the group.

split-camp.title: The Split Camp

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

split-camp.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: The group splits.
Some families stay near the river. Others prepare to follow the old migration route.
Your father stands between the two camps, torn between fear and hope.
A divided group has fewer hands for every task.

choices.text: Negotiate a shared plan between both camps.
choices.hint: Cooperation may repair the split.

choices.text: Focus only on the farming camp.
choices.hint: Commitment helps farming, but isolation is risky.

choices.text: Leave with the migrating camp to protect family unity.
choices.hint: This returns to a nomadic path.

chosen-seeds.title: Choosing the Strongest

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

chosen-seeds.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: With Nima's help, you choose seeds from the plants that grow tallest and return fastest.
You do not know it yet, but this is a step toward domestication.
Humans are beginning to shape which plants survive.

choices.text: Plant only the strongest seeds.
choices.hint: This supports domestication.

choices.text: Save some seeds in case the first planting fails.
choices.hint: Storage can protect against risk.

choices.text: Teach others how to identify strong seeds.
choices.hint: Shared knowledge supports the group.

nima-speaks.title: Nima's Voice

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

nima-speaks.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: Nima stands beside you at the fire.
She says, 'We followed the herds because the land taught us to move. Perhaps now the river is teaching us to stay.'
For the first time, the group listens without laughter.

choices.text: Ask for a full season to test farming.
choices.hint: A longer trial may allow real results.

choices.text: Ask only a few families to stay and test the idea.
choices.hint: A smaller test lowers risk but may divide the group.

choices.text: Ask everyone to begin building a settlement.
choices.hint: Full commitment can bring reward or disaster.

thorn-barrier.title: The Thorn Fence

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

thorn-barrier.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: You and Sela drag thorn branches through the mud until your arms ache.
The barrier is ugly, but it works. In the morning, animal tracks circle the field instead of crossing it.
For the first time, the plants survive the night.

choices.text: Teach others how to protect the field.
choices.hint: Shared labor strengthens the settlement.

choices.text: Expand the barrier before expanding the field.
choices.hint: Careful planning may prevent crop loss.

choices.text: Focus only on the field and neglect hunting.
choices.hint: Too much reliance on crops may be dangerous.

hunter-guards.title: Hunters at the Field

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

hunter-guards.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: The hunters agree to guard the field, but they grumble.
One says, 'Spears are for herds, not plants.'
By morning, even the proudest hunter admits the field may be worth protecting.

choices.text: Thank the hunters and keep hunting as part of the plan.
choices.hint: Balance may produce a safer path.

choices.text: Suggest that some hunters become field guards.
choices.hint: This moves toward job specialization.

choices.text: Say farming matters more than hunting now.
choices.hint: Disrespecting old roles may cause conflict.

camp-field.title: A Field Near the Fires

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

camp-field.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: You move the test field closer to camp.
It is easier to protect, but the soil is not as rich as the riverbank.
Every choice has a tradeoff.

choices.text: Carry river mud to improve the camp field.
choices.hint: Labor can modify the environment.

choices.text: Move camp closer to the fertile riverbank.
choices.hint: This improves access but increases flood risk.

choices.text: Keep two small fields in different places.
choices.hint: Diversifying risk may help survival.

revealed-field.title: The Hidden Field Revealed

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

revealed-field.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: You lead your father, Nima, and several others through the reeds.
When they see the growing plants, no one speaks at first.
Your father touches one leaf as if it might disappear.

choices.text: Admit you hid the field because you feared they would leave.
choices.hint: Honesty may rebuild trust.

choices.text: Use the proof to demand that the group stay.
choices.hint: Proof without humility may create conflict.

choices.text: Ask the group to help protect the field until harvest.
choices.hint: Cooperation may lead toward settlement.

wait-harvest.title: Waiting Too Long

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

wait-harvest.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: You wait for the plants to mature.
The proof grows stronger, but the group prepares to migrate.
A good idea can fail if it arrives too late.

choices.text: Reveal the field before the group leaves.
choices.hint: There is still time to change direction.

choices.text: Stay behind alone to finish the harvest.
choices.hint: Isolation makes survival dangerous.

choices.text: Cut the young plants and bring them as proof.
choices.hint: This may convince people, but destroys the harvest.

father-orun.title: Your Father's Silence

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

father-orun.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: Your father stands over the seedlings in silence.
He has always trusted movement, tracks, and spears.
Now he sees Tavi watering plants with careful hands.

choices.text: Ask your father to support a temporary settlement.
choices.hint: Family support may shift the council.

choices.text: Ask your father to organize hunters while others plant.
choices.hint: Balanced labor may help survival.

choices.text: Challenge your father's belief in the old ways.
choices.hint: Conflict with tradition may split support.

daily-rhythm.title: A New Rhythm

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

daily-rhythm.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: Each morning, Tavi waters the plants. Each afternoon, you clear weeds from the soil.
The days begin to feel different. Not easier, but more predictable.
A settled life begins with repeated tasks.

choices.text: Invite others into the routine.
choices.hint: Shared routines support settlement.

choices.text: Create a place to store seeds and tools.
choices.hint: Storage supports future planning.

choices.text: Focus on the field and stop moving with hunters.
choices.hint: Commitment may help farming but risk hunger.

hunter-pride.title: Pride of the Hunters

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

hunter-pride.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: The hunters celebrate the meat they bring back.
For several days, your group eats better. People praise the old ways again.
But when the meat is gone, hunger returns.

choices.text: Accept that hunting and gathering remain best for now.
choices.hint: This may lead toward a neutral ending.

choices.text: Use full stomachs as a chance to experiment.
choices.hint: Temporary security can allow innovation.

choices.text: Push the group to follow the herds even farther.
choices.hint: This may become dangerous.

father-disagrees.title: A Crack in the Family

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

father-disagrees.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: Your father shakes his head. 'You think hunger is new?' he says. 'You think fear is new?'
His words sting because you know he is afraid for you.
The future is pulling one way. Your family is pulling another.

choices.text: Listen to your father and look for compromise.
choices.hint: Compromise may preserve trust.

choices.text: Choose the river even if your father refuses.
choices.hint: This may create a split.

choices.text: Abandon the farming idea to keep peace.
choices.hint: This may preserve family unity but slow change.

turn-back.title: The Debate to Turn Back

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

turn-back.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: You ask the group to turn back toward the river.
Some are too tired to argue. Others fear losing the herd tracks.
The choice is no longer between comfort and danger. It is between two dangers.

choices.text: Return to the river as a whole group.
choices.hint: The agricultural path can still continue.

choices.text: Send a small group back to test the river.
choices.hint: This lowers risk but divides people.

choices.text: Continue migration despite doubts.
choices.hint: This continues the risky nomadic route.

unknown-plains.title: The Unknown Plains

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

unknown-plains.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: The plains stretch farther than anyone expected.
There are tracks, but no herd. Clouds gather without rain.
The group still moves because movement is what it knows.

choices.text: Organize scouts to search in different directions.
choices.hint: Better organization may help survival.

choices.text: Turn back before the weakest collapse.
choices.hint: Returning may save the group.

choices.text: Keep pushing forward and hope for a herd.
choices.hint: Hope without evidence can be dangerous.

silent-hunger.title: Silent Hunger

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

silent-hunger.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: You say nothing.
The group keeps moving, and hunger settles into everyone like cold rain.
Tavi no longer complains. That frightens you most.

choices.text: Finally speak up and ask to return to the river.
choices.hint: There may still be time.

choices.text: Help gather whatever food can be found.
choices.hint: Adaptation may support survival.

choices.text: Continue in silence.
choices.hint: This may lead toward disaster.

test-field.title: The First Field

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

test-field.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: You press chosen seeds into the best soil.
Sela spaces them apart. Tavi waters them carefully.
Each seed feels like a promise, but promises can fail.

choices.text: Create a watering routine using river water.
choices.hint: This may lead toward irrigation.

choices.text: Plant as many seeds as possible to increase the harvest.
choices.hint: Rapid expansion may be risky.

choices.text: Keep hunting while the field grows.
choices.hint: Balance may protect the group.

between-two-worlds.title: Between Two Worlds

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

between-two-worlds.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: The group returns to the river when food is available, then moves when the land becomes uncertain.
You plant a little, gather a little, and hunt when you can.
This is not a revolution yet. It is a slow experiment.

choices.text: Keep this mixed lifestyle for another season.
choices.hint: This may lead toward a neutral ending.

choices.text: Use the pattern to prepare for settlement.
choices.hint: This may lead toward stronger farming.

choices.text: Give up farming and return fully to migration.
choices.hint: This preserves the old ways.

first-settlement.title: The First Settlement

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

first-settlement.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: The group begins building stronger shelters.
Stones circle the fires. Storage pits are dug into the ground.
The camp starts to look less like a stopping place and more like a home.

choices.text: Build on higher ground away from floodwater.
choices.hint: Good geography supports settlement.

choices.text: Build close to the river for easy water access.
choices.hint: Convenience may increase flood risk.

choices.text: Build shelters around shared storage pits.
choices.hint: Storage may support surplus and planning.

planned-growth.title: Slow Growth

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

planned-growth.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: You choose a smaller field than some people want.
You leave space between plants, save seeds, and keep hunters active.
The plan feels slow, but the plants grow stronger.

choices.text: Develop irrigation channels before expanding.
choices.hint: Water control may lead toward a strong settlement.

choices.text: Train more people in seed selection and planting.
choices.hint: Shared knowledge strengthens the community.

choices.text: Prepare for a harvest gathering to unite the group.
choices.hint: Shared culture can support community.

hunt-and-field.title: Between Spear and Seed

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

hunt-and-field.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: Some people hunt. Some tend the field. Some gather wild foods.
The group is stretched thin, but no single failure can destroy everyone.
Change does not have to happen all at once.

choices.text: Keep the balanced system for another season.
choices.hint: This may lead toward a neutral ending.

choices.text: Use the balance to prepare for full settlement.
choices.hint: This may lead toward a good ending.

choices.text: Let each family choose its own path.
choices.hint: Freedom may reduce conflict or weaken cooperation.

seed-storage.title: Food for Tomorrow

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

seed-storage.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: You dig lined pits and store seeds in dry baskets.
For people used to eating what they find, saving food feels strange.
Tavi asks why he cannot eat the seeds now. You tell him, 'Because tomorrow is hungry too.'

choices.text: Create rules for shared seed storage.
choices.hint: Fair resource management builds trust.

choices.text: Let each family store its own seeds.
choices.hint: This protects independence but may increase inequality.

choices.text: Use most seeds now to feed hungry children.
choices.hint: Immediate survival may weaken the future harvest.

high-ground.title: Above the Flood Line

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

high-ground.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: You place shelters above the old flood marks.
The river is farther away, but the camp feels safer.
When rain comes, people watch the water rise without panic.

choices.text: Connect the camp to the fields with paths and storage areas.
choices.hint: Organization supports settled life.

choices.text: Dig channels to bring water toward the crops.
choices.hint: This develops irrigation.

choices.text: Focus on barriers around the camp and fields.
choices.hint: Protection matters as settlement grows.

riverbank-camp.title: Too Close to the Water

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

riverbank-camp.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: The river makes life easier at first. Water is close. Crops are close. Fish are close.
Then heavy rain falls in hills beyond sight. By morning, the river rises like an animal waking hungry.

choices.text: Move the shelters to higher ground immediately.
choices.hint: Adapting quickly may prevent disaster.

choices.text: Build barriers and hope the water stops rising.
choices.hint: This may work, but it is risky.

choices.text: Stay close to protect the fields.
choices.hint: Protecting crops over people may be dangerous.

first-channels.title: The First Channels

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

first-channels.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: You and the others dig shallow paths through the mud.
At first, the water wanders. Then it follows the channels toward the field.
The group cheers as the river begins to work with you.

choices.text: Expand the channels slowly and maintain them together.
choices.hint: Cooperation may create reliable agriculture.

choices.text: Expand too quickly before learning the water flow.
choices.hint: Poor planning may cause damage.

choices.text: Use channels only for one field and keep hunting too.
choices.hint: This creates a mixed survival strategy.

fair-roles.title: A Fair Rotation

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

fair-roles.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: Nima helps create a work rotation.
No family guards the field every night. No hunter is forced to stop hunting forever.
People still argue, but the rules give them words to argue with instead of fists.

choices.text: Apply the same fairness to stored food.
choices.hint: Fair systems can reinforce each other.

choices.text: Use the rotation to expand the settlement.
choices.hint: Organized labor supports complexity.

choices.text: Let the system weaken when powerful families complain.
choices.hint: Unequal power may grow.

resource-jealousy.title: Seeds of Jealousy

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

resource-jealousy.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: Some families begin to claim the best tools, best storage pits, and best sleeping places.
The same food that creates security also creates power.
You see the first signs of hierarchy forming around stored grain.

choices.text: Call a meeting to create fair storage rules.
choices.hint: It may still be possible to rebuild trust.

choices.text: Let powerful families control the surplus.
choices.hint: This may create order with inequality.

choices.text: Steal grain for hungry families at night.
choices.hint: This may help briefly but damage trust.

nima-organizes.title: Nima Organizes the Work

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

nima-organizes.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: Nima assigns work based on strength, skill, and need.
Hunters still hunt. Children gather fuel. Skilled hands shape tools. Careful eyes watch the fields.
The group begins to feel like something more organized than a traveling band.

choices.text: Use this organization to build irrigation.
choices.hint: Organized labor can support public works.

choices.text: Use this organization to create shared storage.
choices.hint: Shared systems can protect surplus.

choices.text: Depend too heavily on Nima alone.
choices.hint: A system must outlast one leader.

shared-plan.title: The Shared Plan

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

shared-plan.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: You move between the camps carrying messages.
The hunters agree to return with meat when they can. The farming families agree to share any harvest.
It is not trust yet, but it is a bridge.

choices.text: Keep both camps connected through shared storage.
choices.hint: Shared resources may preserve unity.

choices.text: Invite the hunters back for the harvest gathering.
choices.hint: Community rituals may repair trust.

choices.text: Let the camps drift apart after the agreement.
choices.hint: Weak ties may lead to a mixed outcome.

small-settlement.title: Too Few Hands

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

small-settlement.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: The farming camp works hard, but there are not enough hands.
When animals come, there are too few guards. When weeds spread, there are too few workers.
A new way of life cannot grow easily from isolation.

choices.text: Ask the migrating families to return.
choices.hint: Rebuilding cooperation may help.

choices.text: Reduce the field to what the small group can manage.
choices.hint: Smaller plans may survive.

choices.text: Keep expanding despite too few workers.
choices.hint: Overextension may lead to failure.

nomadic-balance.title: The Old Ways Continue

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

nomadic-balance.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: Your group survives by moving carefully, hunting wisely, and gathering what the land offers.
Tavi survives, though some nights he still goes to sleep hungry.
You think often of the river, but the group remains a band of nomads.

choices.text: Accept survival without full settlement.
choices.hint: This leads to a neutral ending.

choices.text: Return to the river each year for seasonal planting.
choices.hint: This leads to a mixed ending.

choices.text: Teach children both hunting skills and seed knowledge.
choices.hint: This preserves change without full revolution.

water-routine.title: Carrying the River

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

water-routine.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: Every morning, people carry water from the river to the field.
At first, the work feels manageable. Then the sun grows hotter.
The plants need more water than arms can easily carry.

choices.text: Dig narrow channels from the river toward the field.
choices.hint: This leads toward irrigation.

choices.text: Assign more people to carry water.
choices.hint: More labor may help, but creates strain.

choices.text: Reduce the field size to match available water.
choices.hint: Careful limits may prevent collapse.

too-many-seeds.title: Too Much Hope

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

too-many-seeds.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: You plant every seed you can find.
At first, the field looks full of promise. Then the plants crowd one another. The soil dries faster.
Hope has become heavy.

choices.text: Thin the field and save the strongest plants.
choices.hint: Correcting mistakes may protect the harvest.

choices.text: Demand more water and more labor.
choices.hint: Pressure on the group may create resentment.

choices.text: Ignore the warning signs.
choices.hint: Ignoring environmental limits can lead to failure.

crop-dependence.title: All Eyes on the Field

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

crop-dependence.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: The hunters go out less often. Gatherers stay near camp.
The crops become more than food. They become hope, fear, and argument.
If they fail, there is no backup.

choices.text: Restore hunting and gathering as backup.
choices.hint: Balance may protect survival.

choices.text: Double down on farming despite the risk.
choices.hint: Dependence can be dangerous.

choices.text: Build storage and irrigation to reduce risk.
choices.hint: Planning may save the settlement.

soil-work.title: Working the Soil

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

soil-work.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: You carry baskets of dark river mud to the weaker field near camp.
It is exhausting work, but the soil improves.
Your people are not just living in the environment now. They are changing it.

choices.text: Use this method carefully for small fields.
choices.hint: Small changes may support success.

choices.text: Demand that everyone carry soil every day.
choices.hint: Too much forced labor may create conflict.

choices.text: Move the settlement closer to better soil.
choices.hint: This improves farming but increases flood risk.

two-fields.title: Two Fields, Two Risks

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

two-fields.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: One field grows near camp. The other grows near the riverbank.
Neither is perfect. Together, they give the group more than one chance.
Risk feels different when it is spread out.

choices.text: Keep both fields small and protected.
choices.hint: Careful planning supports survival.

choices.text: Focus only on the river field because it grows faster.
choices.hint: Better soil may bring greater flood risk.

choices.text: Let families control different fields.
choices.hint: This may protect independence but increase division.

scout-search.title: Scouts in the Dry Grass

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

scout-search.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: Scouts travel in three directions.
One finds dry tracks. One finds bitter roots. One does not return until nightfall, shaking with exhaustion.
The land is not empty, but it is not generous.

choices.text: Turn back toward the river with this evidence.
choices.hint: Evidence can change direction.

choices.text: Continue carefully with smaller scouting groups.
choices.hint: This leads toward nomadic survival.

choices.text: Ignore the scouts and push forward.
choices.hint: Ignoring evidence may lead to disaster.

migration-crisis.title: When Movement Fails

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

migration-crisis.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: The group moves, but the land gives little.
Tavi collapses beside a dry stream. Your father lifts him, but his own legs shake.
The old ways have not betrayed you. The environment has changed faster than your plans.

choices.text: Turn back in desperation.
choices.hint: There may still be a path back.

choices.text: Break into small groups to search for food.
choices.hint: Division in crisis is dangerous.

choices.text: Keep moving until a herd appears.
choices.hint: This leads toward a bad ending.

season-balance.title: A Season of Balance

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

season-balance.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: The group hunts, gathers, and plants small fields near the river.
No one is fully satisfied, but no one is fully ignored.
The future arrives slowly, one compromise at a time.

choices.text: Choose the balanced way of life as the group's future.
choices.hint: This leads to a neutral ending.

choices.text: Use balance as preparation for full settlement.
choices.hint: This may lead toward a good ending.

choices.text: Let the balance fall apart when pressure rises.
choices.hint: Weak cooperation may limit success.

loose-community.title: Everyone Chooses Alone

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

loose-community.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: Each family chooses its own path.
Some plant. Some hunt. Some store food privately. Some share.
There is less arguing, but also less trust.

choices.text: Accept a loose mixed lifestyle.
choices.hint: This leads to a neutral ending.

choices.text: Rebuild common rules before harvest.
choices.hint: Trust may still return.

choices.text: Let stronger families take control.
choices.hint: This may lead to inequality.

fair-storage.title: Rules for the Storehouse

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

fair-storage.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: The group gathers beside the storage pits.
Nima helps create rules. Seeds for planting are separate from food for eating. Families in need will receive help.
Surplus becomes less frightening when people trust how it will be used.

choices.text: Use shared storage to support irrigation work.
choices.hint: Surplus can support community projects.

choices.text: Use shared storage to survive winter cautiously.
choices.hint: Careful resource management may succeed.

choices.text: Let the rules weaken under pressure from powerful families.
choices.hint: Inequality may grow.

family-storage.title: Private Baskets

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

family-storage.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: Each family stores its own seeds.
At first, this feels fair. Then some baskets fill while others stay nearly empty.
A settled life is creating new questions about ownership and responsibility.

choices.text: Create emergency sharing rules.
choices.hint: The group may still build trust.

choices.text: Accept unequal storage as the price of independence.
choices.hint: This leads to a neutral ending.

choices.text: Let hunger turn into theft and blame.
choices.hint: Conflict may worsen.

hard-sacrifice.title: Seeds or Children

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

hard-sacrifice.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: The children are hungry now. The seeds are food now too.
But every seed eaten is a plant that will not grow later.
Survival asks a cruel question. Who is tomorrow for if today does not live?

choices.text: Share some seeds but protect enough for planting.
choices.hint: A painful balance may work.

choices.text: Eat nearly all the seeds.
choices.hint: The group may survive but lose the farming future.

choices.text: Refuse to share seeds with hungry families.
choices.hint: Desperation may damage trust.

storage-paths.title: Paths, Pits, and Plans

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

storage-paths.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: Paths now connect shelters, fields, water, and storage pits.
Children know where tools belong. Hunters know when the field must be guarded.
The camp feels less temporary with each sunrise.

choices.text: Hold a harvest council to plan the next season.
choices.hint: Planning can secure the settlement.

choices.text: Build irrigation before the next dry season.
choices.hint: Water control can strengthen agriculture.

choices.text: Expand too quickly now that things seem stable.
choices.hint: Confidence can become carelessness.

protected-settlement.title: Barriers and Watchfires

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

protected-settlement.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: The group builds barriers around fields and lights watchfires near storage.
Protection brings comfort, but also makes the camp feel permanent.
Some people like that. Others feel trapped.

choices.text: Use protection to support a stable settlement.
choices.hint: Security may support civilization.

choices.text: Keep exits open for seasonal migration.
choices.hint: This preserves flexibility.

choices.text: Let fear of outsiders control every decision.
choices.hint: Fear may create stability with limits.

flood-test.title: The Flood Test

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

flood-test.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: The barriers hold for one night.
By morning, water presses against them, heavy and brown.
The group must decide whether pride matters more than safety.

choices.text: Retreat to high ground with seeds and tools.
choices.hint: Adapting may save the group.

choices.text: Stay and fight the flood.
choices.hint: This is dangerous.

choices.text: Save the children first and abandon part of the field.
choices.hint: A painful choice may preserve the future.

flood-loss.title: Water Over the Fields

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

flood-loss.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: The river breaks through the camp.
Baskets float away. Young plants vanish under brown water.
The river that promised life has reminded everyone that it cannot be controlled without knowledge.

choices.text: Rebuild on higher ground with what remains.
choices.hint: This leads to a cautious mixed future.

choices.text: Blame one another until the group divides.
choices.hint: Conflict after disaster may destroy the settlement.

choices.text: Learn from the flood and build channels properly.
choices.hint: Failure can still teach.

irrigation-system.title: Mastering the River

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

irrigation-system.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: The channels deepen. Water moves from the river to the fields.
People take turns clearing mud and repairing the banks.
The river is still powerful, but now the group has learned to work with it.

choices.text: Share the water and storage fairly.
choices.hint: This leads to a good ending.

choices.text: Let a few families control the best watered fields.
choices.hint: This leads to a mixed ending.

choices.text: Expand beyond what the channels can support.
choices.hint: Overexpansion may cause failure.

teaching-circle.title: The Teaching Circle

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

teaching-circle.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: You teach children and adults how to choose strong seeds, clear weeds, and watch the soil.
Knowledge no longer lives only in your hands.
A community grows stronger when useful knowledge is shared.

choices.text: Combine teaching with shared storage rules.
choices.hint: Knowledge and fairness can support success.

choices.text: Train specialists for planting, tools, and guarding.
choices.hint: This supports job specialization.

choices.text: Keep the best knowledge for your own family.
choices.hint: Private control may increase inequality.

harvest-gathering.title: The Harvest Gathering

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

harvest-gathering.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: The first harvest is small, but real.
People gather around baskets of grain. Tavi reaches toward them, and for once, no one pulls his hand away.
The group has not escaped hunger forever, but it has created a new possibility.

choices.text: Save the strongest seeds for next season.
choices.hint: Planning may secure the future.

choices.text: Eat most of the harvest in celebration.
choices.hint: Joy now may create risk later.

choices.text: Use the harvest to bring the split camp back together.
choices.hint: Food may repair trust.

labor-strain.title: Hands That Ache

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

labor-strain.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: People carry water, pull weeds, gather wood, and guard the fields.
Some begin to say farming is just another kind of hunger.
If the work feels unfair, the settlement will not hold.

choices.text: Create fair work rotations.
choices.hint: Fairness may restore trust.

choices.text: Force tired families to keep working.
choices.hint: Forced labor may break the group.

choices.text: Reduce the field and return to balance.
choices.hint: Smaller goals may survive.

crop-failure-path.title: The Field Turns Yellow

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

crop-failure-path.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: The plants yellow in the sun.
Too many seeds, too little water, too little planning.
People stare at the field as if anger alone can make it green again.

choices.text: Admit the mistake and return to balanced survival.
choices.hint: This leads to a neutral ending.

choices.text: Blame others and protect your own family's food.
choices.hint: Blame may destroy cooperation.

choices.text: Try to rebuild with irrigation and smaller fields.
choices.hint: Learning from failure may still help.

elite-control.title: The First Powerful Families

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

elite-control.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: The strongest families begin guarding the best fields and fullest storage pits.
They promise order, but others whisper that surplus has become a weapon.
A settled life has created new power.

choices.text: Accept order with inequality.
choices.hint: This leads to a neutral ending.

choices.text: Challenge the powerful families and restore shared rules.
choices.hint: Trust may still be rebuilt.

choices.text: Let hunger and anger break into open conflict.
choices.hint: This may lead to a bad ending.

grain-theft.title: Night at the Storage Pit

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

grain-theft.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: Someone steals grain in the dark.
By morning, accusations move faster than truth. Families grip tools as if they are weapons.
Stored food was meant to protect the group. Now it threatens to divide it.

choices.text: Investigate calmly and create sharing rules.
choices.hint: Justice may restore trust.

choices.text: Punish a weak family without proof.
choices.hint: Unfair punishment may destroy unity.

choices.text: End shared storage and return to family control.
choices.hint: This leads to a mixed outcome.

leader-strain.title: Too Much on Nima

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

leader-strain.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: Everyone brings problems to Nima.
Where should water go? Who should guard the field? Who receives grain first?
Even the wisest elder cannot become the whole system.

choices.text: Create shared rules so the system outlasts one leader.
choices.hint: Institutions may support settlement.

choices.text: Let Nima decide everything.
choices.hint: This may keep order but limit fairness.

choices.text: Ignore Nima when her decisions become difficult.
choices.hint: Losing trusted leadership may break the group.

cut-proof.title: Proof in Your Hands

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

cut-proof.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: You cut the young plants and carry them to camp.
People finally believe you, but the hidden harvest is gone.
Proof has a cost.

choices.text: Use the proof to begin again with group support.
choices.hint: The idea may survive.

choices.text: Accept that the group must move for now.
choices.hint: Knowledge may carry forward.

choices.text: Blame the group for making you destroy the plants.
choices.hint: Anger may divide people.

alone-river.title: Alone at the River

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

alone-river.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: You stay behind when the group leaves.
For two days, the plants keep you company. On the third, you hear animals in the reeds at night.
A new idea is not enough when you must face the land alone.

choices.text: Try to find the group and return.
choices.hint: You may survive with hard-earned knowledge.

choices.text: Keep guarding the field alone.
choices.hint: Isolation is dangerous.

choices.text: Signal with smoke and hope someone comes back.
choices.hint: Cooperation may still save the idea.

final-harvest-council.title: The Harvest Council

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

final-harvest-council.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: The harvest is not huge, but it is real.
There is food for now and seeds for later, if people choose carefully.
The group gathers beside the storage pits. This decision will shape the future.

choices.text: Share food fairly, save seeds, and prepare the next planting.
choices.hint: This leads to a good ending.

choices.text: Save some seeds while continuing hunting and gathering.
choices.hint: This leads to a neutral ending.

choices.text: Let powerful families control most of the grain.
choices.hint: This leads to a mixed ending.

neutral-old-ways.title: Survival Without Change

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

neutral-old-ways.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: Your group continues moving with the seasons.
You hunt, gather, track weather, and follow the land's signals as your ancestors did.
Tavi survives, though hunger still visits. Sometimes you dream of the river plants and wonder what might have been.
neutral-old-ways.teachingSummary: This ending shows that Paleolithic hunter-gatherers were skilled at adapting to their environments. Not all groups adopted agriculture immediately, and survival could continue through nomadic movement and environmental knowledge.
neutral-old-ways.keyTermReview: Vocabulary review: This adventure connects Paleolithic nomadic life to the Neolithic Revolution. Students see how domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus food, river valleys, and job specialization helped create the foundations of civilization.
reflectionQuestions: How did Kara's choices reflect the risks and rewards of the Neolithic Revolution?
Which key term best explains your ending? Use evidence from the adventure.
How did the environment shape the choices available to Kara's group?
Was agriculture worth the risk for Kara's people? Explain using details from your path.
What tradeoff did Kara's group face between survival, tradition, and change?

neutral-seasonal.title: Between Two Worlds

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

neutral-seasonal.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: Your group returns to the river each year.
You plant small fields, gather wild foods, and still follow herds when needed.
The change is slow, but it is real. Your people live between the Paleolithic past and the Neolithic future.
neutral-seasonal.teachingSummary: This ending shows that the shift to agriculture was gradual. Some communities blended hunting, gathering, seasonal movement, and early farming before becoming fully settled.
neutral-seasonal.keyTermReview: Vocabulary review: This adventure connects Paleolithic nomadic life to the Neolithic Revolution. Students see how domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus food, river valleys, and job specialization helped create the foundations of civilization.
reflectionQuestions: How did Kara's choices reflect the risks and rewards of the Neolithic Revolution?
Which key term best explains your ending? Use evidence from the adventure.
How did the environment shape the choices available to Kara's group?
Was agriculture worth the risk for Kara's people? Explain using details from your path.
What tradeoff did Kara's group face between survival, tradition, and change?

neutral-knowledge.title: Knowledge Carried Forward

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

neutral-knowledge.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: The group does not settle, but it does not forget.
Children learn which seeds grow best, where the river leaves rich soil, and why stored food matters.
The future has not arrived yet, but someone will be ready when it does.
neutral-knowledge.teachingSummary: This ending shows that knowledge can spread before a full revolution occurs. Observation, experimentation, and shared memory helped prepare humans for later agricultural change.
neutral-knowledge.keyTermReview: Vocabulary review: This adventure connects Paleolithic nomadic life to the Neolithic Revolution. Students see how domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus food, river valleys, and job specialization helped create the foundations of civilization.
reflectionQuestions: How did Kara's choices reflect the risks and rewards of the Neolithic Revolution?
Which key term best explains your ending? Use evidence from the adventure.
How did the environment shape the choices available to Kara's group?
Was agriculture worth the risk for Kara's people? Explain using details from your path.

neutral-unequal.title: Order With Unequal Baskets

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

neutral-unequal.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: The settlement survives, but not everyone benefits equally.
Some families control better fields and fuller storage pits. Others depend on their decisions.
Agriculture brings stability, but it also begins to create new differences in power and status.
neutral-unequal.teachingSummary: This ending shows that agriculture and surplus could create both stability and inequality. Settled life helped communities grow, but it could also lead to social hierarchy.
neutral-unequal.keyTermReview: Vocabulary review: This adventure connects Paleolithic nomadic life to the Neolithic Revolution. Students see how domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus food, river valleys, and job specialization helped create the foundations of civilization.
reflectionQuestions: How did Kara's choices reflect the risks and rewards of the Neolithic Revolution?
Which key term best explains your ending? Use evidence from the adventure.
How did the environment shape the choices available to Kara's group?
Was agriculture worth the risk for Kara's people? Explain using details from your path.
What tradeoff did Kara's group face between survival, tradition, and change?

good-civilization.title: Seeds of Civilization

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

good-civilization.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: The next season begins with planning instead of panic.
Channels carry river water toward the fields. Seeds are saved, planted, and protected. Tavi grows stronger.
Some people farm, some hunt, some shape tools, and some guard the storage pits.
Your people are no longer only surviving. They are building the first pieces of settled life.
good-civilization.teachingSummary: This ending shows how the Neolithic Revolution helped humans create agriculture, surplus food, permanent settlements, irrigation, job specialization, and the foundations of civilization.
good-civilization.keyTermReview: Vocabulary review: This adventure connects Paleolithic nomadic life to the Neolithic Revolution. Students see how domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus food, river valleys, and job specialization helped create the foundations of civilization.
reflectionQuestions: How did Kara's choices reflect the risks and rewards of the Neolithic Revolution?
Which key term best explains your ending? Use evidence from the adventure.
How did the environment shape the choices available to Kara's group?
Was agriculture worth the risk for Kara's people? Explain using details from your path.
What tradeoff did Kara's group face between survival, tradition, and change?

bad-collapse.title: The Cost of Failure

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

bad-collapse.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: The fields weaken. Stored food disappears into private shelters. The hunters leave angry, and the children grow hungry again.
When the river floods, no one has prepared safe storage or working channels.
You discovered a new way to live, but without planning and cooperation, the new way collapses.
bad-collapse.teachingSummary: This ending shows that agriculture was risky. Early farming required environmental knowledge, cooperation, water control, resource management, and trust.
bad-collapse.keyTermReview: Vocabulary review: This adventure connects Paleolithic nomadic life to the Neolithic Revolution. Students see how domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus food, river valleys, and job specialization helped create the foundations of civilization.
reflectionQuestions: How did Kara's choices reflect the risks and rewards of the Neolithic Revolution?
Which key term best explains your ending? Use evidence from the adventure.
How did the environment shape the choices available to Kara's group?
Was agriculture worth the risk for Kara's people? Explain using details from your path.

bad-starvation.title: No Herd on the Horizon

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

bad-starvation.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: The group keeps walking, waiting for the herd to appear.
It never does.
By the time the elders speak of turning back, Tavi can no longer stand without help. The old ways needed land that could still provide, and this land has failed you.
bad-starvation.teachingSummary: This ending shows that Paleolithic survival depended on available resources and successful adaptation. When environments changed, groups that could not adjust faced serious danger.
bad-starvation.keyTermReview: Vocabulary review: This adventure connects Paleolithic nomadic life to the Neolithic Revolution. Students see how domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus food, river valleys, and job specialization helped create the foundations of civilization.
reflectionQuestions: How did Kara's choices reflect the risks and rewards of the Neolithic Revolution?

How did the environment shape the choices available to Kara's group?

bad-scattered.title: Scattered Fires

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

bad-scattered.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: The group breaks apart to search for food.
Small fires appear across the dark plain, each one weaker than the full campfire used to be.
Without cooperation, every danger grows larger.
bad-scattered.teachingSummary: This ending shows why cooperation mattered for early human survival. Whether hunting, gathering, or farming, humans depended on group organization and shared support.
bad-scattered.keyTermReview: Vocabulary review: This adventure connects Paleolithic nomadic life to the Neolithic Revolution. Students see how domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus food, river valleys, and job specialization helped create the foundations of civilization.
reflectionQuestions: How did Kara's choices reflect the risks and rewards of the Neolithic Revolution?

bad-alone.title: Alone With the Field

image.alt: Seeds, stone tools, and a river valley representing the shift from hunting and gathering to farming

bad-alone.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Paleolithic, Neolithic Revolution, nomad, domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus, river valley, civilization, and job specialization.
body: You guard the field alone until your food runs out.
The plants still grow, but they cannot gather water, build shelter, or watch the reeds at night.
A discovery can begin with one person, but survival requires a community.
bad-alone.teachingSummary: This ending shows that innovation alone was not enough. Early agriculture required cooperation, labor, protection, and shared planning.
bad-alone.keyTermReview: Vocabulary review: This adventure connects Paleolithic nomadic life to the Neolithic Revolution. Students see how domestication, agriculture, irrigation, surplus food, river valleys, and job specialization helped create the foundations of civilization.
reflectionQuestions: How did Kara's choices reflect the risks and rewards of the Neolithic Revolution?
